title: Compressor Hums But Will Not Start
complaint: Outdoor unit hums loudly but the compressor does not start. Condenser fan runs normally.
initialData: Contactor is pulled in (24V at coil)
Condenser fan running at normal speed
Compressor hums for 3–5 seconds then trips on overload

s1.prompt: The contactor is closed and the fan runs. The compressor hums but trips. What is the most likely cause?

s1-wrong.prompt: The contactor is confirmed closed (fan runs). Low charge would not cause humming. A humming motor that cannot start points to the capacitor.
s1-wrong.feedback: Humming + trips on overload = motor trying but cannot turn = capacitor failure.

s2.prompt: Before testing the capacitor, what must you do?

options.label: Test it live with the system running
Discharge it with a 10kΩ resistor after LOTO

s2-wrong.prompt: Testing a live capacitor without discharge is dangerous — it can hold a lethal charge even after power is removed.
s2-wrong.feedback: Always discharge capacitors before handling. Use a 10kΩ, 5W resistor across the terminals.

s2-partial.prompt: Replacing without testing is acceptable in the field but wastes parts if the capacitor is good. What is the correct test?

options.label: Discharge after LOTO, measure capacitance in microfarads

s3.prompt: After LOTO and discharge, you measure the dual-run capacitor: HERM reads 28 µF (rated 45 µF), FAN reads 7.5 µF (rated 7.5 µF). What do you do?

options.label: The capacitor is fine — look elsewhere

s3-wrong.prompt: The HERM section reads 28 µF vs. rated 45 µF — that is 38% low, well outside the ±6% tolerance. The dual-run capacitor must be replaced as a unit.
s3-wrong.feedback: Dual-run capacitors are replaced as a complete unit. A weak HERM section causes the compressor to draw excess current and trip.

pass.prompt: Correct. Replacing the dual-run capacitor restored normal compressor starting. Root cause: weak HERM capacitor section caused insufficient starting torque.
pass.feedback: Weak run capacitor → insufficient phase shift → motor cannot develop starting torque → hums and trips on thermal overload.
